annotations.label: Fermat's little theorem (a^p − a form)
annotations.note: Int.ModEq.pow_prime_eq_self states n^p ≡ n [ZMOD p] for prime p, exactly the a^p−a form (also ZMod.pow_card and Int.prime_dvd_pow_self_sub).

annotations.label: Example: a=2, p=7 (a^p − a)
annotations.note: This concrete numerical instance (128−2=126=7×18) is not stated in Mathlib, though it is provable by decide/norm_num from the general theorem.

annotations.label: Fermat's little theorem (coprime form)

anchor.snippet: is equivalent to the statement that

annotations.note: Int.ModEq.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one gives a^(p−1) ≡ 1 [ZMOD p] for a coprime to p (with Nat/ZMod variants).

anchors.snippet: is equivalent to the statement that

annotations.label: Example: a=2, p=7 (coprime form)
annotations.note: This concrete numerical instance (64−1=63=7×9) is not stated in Mathlib, though provable by decide from the general theorem.

annotations.label: Fermat's original formulation

anchor.snippet: is any integer not divisible by p

annotations.note: Int.prime_dvd_pow_sub_one states (p:ℤ) ∣ n^(p−1)−1 for n coprime to p, matching Fermat's original divisibility formulation.

annotations.label: Chinese hypothesis and its counterexample
annotations.note: Neither the Chinese hypothesis (2^n ≡ 2 mod n ⇒ n prime) nor its counterexample 341 is stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler's theorem

anchor.snippet: For any modulus n and any integer a coprime to n

annotations.note: Nat.ModEq.pow_totient states a^φ(n) ≡ 1 [MOD n] for a coprime to n (Fermat–Euler totient theorem), with unit version ZMod.pow_totient.

annotations.label: Fermat as special case of Euler
annotations.note: The bridging fact φ(p)=p−1 is Nat.totient_prime, but the explicit statement that Fermat is a special case of Euler is not formalized as a single declaration.

annotations.label: Corollary of Euler's theorem (exponent reduction)

anchor.snippet: For every positive integer n , if the integer a is coprime with n

annotations.note: pow_eq_pow_iff_modEq gives x^m=x^k ↔ m≡k [MOD orderOf x], a refinement, but the exact exponent-reduction-mod-φ(n) corollary is not stated separately.

anchors.snippet: For every positive integer n , if the integer a is coprime with n

annotations.label: Totient of a product of two primes

anchor.snippet: if n = pq is the product of two distinct prime numbers

annotations.note: φ(pq)=(p−1)(q−1) follows from Nat.totient_mul (coprime multiplicativity) plus Nat.totient_prime, but is not a standalone lemma.

annotations.label: Converse fails for Carmichael numbers
annotations.note: Failure of the converse is witnessed by Nat.exists_infinite_pseudoprimes (composite numbers passing the test), but Carmichael numbers specifically are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lehmer's theorem

anchor.snippet: and for all primes q dividing

annotations.note: lucas_primality states that a^(p−1)=1 with a^((p−1)/q)≠1 for all primes q ∣ p−1 implies p prime, exactly Lehmer's converse theorem.

anchors.snippet: and for all primes q dividing

annotations.label: Fermat pseudoprime to base a

anchor.snippet: is called a (Fermat) pseudoprime to base a

annotations.note: Nat.FermatPsp n b defines a Fermat pseudoprime as a composite n with n ∣ b^(n−1)−1, matching the article's definition.

annotations.label: First base-2 pseudoprime (Sarrus, 341)

anchor.snippet: The first pseudoprime to base 2 was found in 1820

annotations.note: The specific fact that 341 is the first base-2 pseudoprime is not stated in Mathlib, though FermatPsp 341 2 is decidable.

annotations.label: Carmichael number
annotations.note: Carmichael numbers are not defined in Mathlib (FermatPsp.lean explicitly notes this); ArithmeticFunction.carmichael is the unrelated Carmichael function.

annotations.label: Prime or Carmichael characterization

anchor.snippet: is either a prime or a Carmichael number

annotations.note: This characterization is not formalized since Carmichael numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: is either a prime or a Carmichael number

annotations.label: Miller–Rabin extension of Fermat's little theorem

anchor.snippet: If p is an odd prime and p − 1 = 2 s d

annotations.note: No Miller–Rabin primality test or its underlying square-root-of-unity refinement of Fermat's theorem is present in Mathlib.
